subject: Physics
q: In the given figure, the block of mass m is dropped from the point 'A'. The expression for kinetic energy of block when it reaches point 'B' is
question: In the given figure, the block of mass m is dropped from the point 'A'. The expression for kinetic energy of block when it reaches point 'B' is
solution:
Here, gain in kinetic energy=loss in potential energy

Now loss in potential energy of particle in falling from height y to y0 is mgy-y0.

So, ΔKE=K-0=-mgy-y0-mgy

Thus, kinetic energy at point B is K=mgy0

q: Two particles of the same mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> are moving in circular orbits because of force, given by <math><mi>F</mi><mi> </mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>16</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>-</mo><msup><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The first particle is at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>, and the second, at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math>. The best estimate for the ratio of kinetic energies of the first and the second particle is closest to
question: Two particles of the same mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> are moving in circular orbits because of force, given by <math><mi>F</mi><mi> </mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>16</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>-</mo><msup><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The first particle is at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>, and the second, at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math>. The best estimate for the ratio of kinetic energies of the first and the second particle is closest to
solution:
Centripetal force is given as Fr=mv2r.

Given in the problem, mV2r=16r+r3  ...i

Kinetic energy is stated as KE0=12mv2.

KE0=1216+r4 (From equationi)

The ratio of kinetic energies of first and second particle is K1K2=16+1216+2562=17272

K1K2 ≃6 ×10-2.

q:
A uniform chain of length 3 m and mass 3 kg overhangs a smooth table with 2 m laying on the table. If K is the kinetic energy of the chain in J as it completely slips off the table, then the value of K is
Take g=10 m s-2
question:
A uniform chain of length 3 m and mass 3 kg overhangs a smooth table with 2 m laying on the table. If K is the kinetic energy of the chain in J as it completely slips off the table, then the value of K is
Take g=10 m s-2
solution:
Apply conservation of energy Ui+Ki=Uf+Kf

−1×10×12+0=−3×10×1.5+KE

K.E.=45−5

K.E.=40 J

subject: Physics
q: A block moving horizontally on a smooth surface with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> splits into two equal parts. If one of the parts moves at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in the same direction, then the fractional change in the kinetic energy will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo></math> ________.
question: A block moving horizontally on a smooth surface with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> splits into two equal parts. If one of the parts moves at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in the same direction, then the fractional change in the kinetic energy will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo></math> ________.
solution:
2m×40=60×m+mv
V=20 m/s
Now ΔKE=KEf-KEi=12 m×(20)2+12 m×(60)2-122 m×(40)2
ΔKE=12 m(800)⇒KEi=122 m×(40)2=12 m×3200
Fractional change =ΔKEKEi=14

subject: Physics
q: A uniform chain of length $2 \mathrm{~m}$ is kept on a table such that a length of $60 \mathrm{~cm}$ hangs freely from the edge of the table. The total mass of the chain is $4 \mathrm{~kg}$. What is the work done in pulling the entire chain on the table?
question: A uniform chain of length $2 \mathrm{~m}$ is kept on a table such that a length of $60 \mathrm{~cm}$ hangs freely from the edge of the table. The total mass of the chain is $4 \mathrm{~kg}$. What is the work done in pulling the entire chain on the table?
solution: Work done $=\mathrm{mgh}=1.2 \times 0.3 \times 10=3.6 \mathrm{~J}$

subject: Physics
q: A small bob $A$ of mass $m$ is attached to a massless rigid rod of length 1 m pivoted at point $P$ and kept at an angle of $60^{\circ}$ with vertical as shown in figure. At distance of 1 m below point $P$, an identical bob $B$ is kept at rest on a smooth horizontal surface that extends to a circular track of radius $R$ as shown in figure. If bob $B$ just manages to complete the circular path of radius $R$ upto a point $Q$ after being hit elastically by $\operatorname{bob} A$, then radius $R$ is $\_\_\_\_$ m.
question: A small bob $A$ of mass $m$ is attached to a massless rigid rod of length 1 m pivoted at point $P$ and kept at an angle of $60^{\circ}$ with vertical as shown in figure. At distance of 1 m below point $P$, an identical bob $B$ is kept at rest on a smooth horizontal surface that extends to a circular track of radius $R$ as shown in figure. If bob $B$ just manages to complete the circular path of radius $R$ upto a point $Q$ after being hit elastically by $\operatorname{bob} A$, then radius $R$ is $\_\_\_\_$ m.
solution:
Bob A swings from $60°$ to vertical. Height drop $h = l(1-\cos 60°) = 1 \times 0.5 = 0.5$ m.
Velocity of A at bottom: $v_A = \sqrt{2gh} = \sqrt{2 \times 10 \times 0.5} = \sqrt{10}$ m/s.
Elastic collision between equal masses: B gets $v_B = \sqrt{10}$ m/s, A stops.
For B to just complete circular loop: $v_B^2 = 5gR$.
$10 = 5 \times 10 \times R \Rightarrow R = \frac{1}{5}$ m.

subject: Physics
q: A bob of mass $m$ is suspended at a point $O$ by a light string of length $l$ and left to perform vertical motion (circular) as shown in figure. Initially, by applying horizontal velocity $v_0$ at the point ' A ', the string becomes slack when, the bob reaches at the point ' D '. The ratio of the kinetic energy of the bob at the points $B$ and $C$ is ______ -.
question: A bob of mass $m$ is suspended at a point $O$ by a light string of length $l$ and left to perform vertical motion (circular) as shown in figure. Initially, by applying horizontal velocity $v_0$ at the point ' A ', the string becomes slack when, the bob reaches at the point ' D '. The ratio of the kinetic energy of the bob at the points $B$ and $C$ is ______ -.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}_{\mathrm{A}}^2=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}_{\mathrm{B}}^2+\mathrm{mgh} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}(5 \mathrm{~g} \ell)=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}_{\mathrm{B}}^2+\mathrm{mg} \frac{\ell}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{5 \mathrm{mg} \ell}{2}-\frac{\mathrm{mg} \ell}{2}=\mathrm{KE}_{\mathrm{B}} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{KE}_{\mathrm{B}}=2 \mathrm{mg} \ell \\ & \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}_{\mathrm{C}}^2=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}_{\mathrm{D}}^2+\mathrm{mg} \frac{\ell}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{KE}_{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mg} \ell+\mathrm{mg} \frac{\ell}{2}=\mathrm{mg} \ell \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{KE}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{KE}_{\mathrm{C}}}=2\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A circular table is rotating with an angular velocity of $\omega \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$ about its axis (see figure). There is a smooth groove along a radial direction on the table. A steel ball is gently placed at a distance of $1 \mathrm{~m}$ on the groove. All the surfaces are smooth. If the radius of the table is $3 \mathrm{~m}$, the radial velocity of the ball w.r.t. the table at the time ball leaves the table is $x \sqrt{2} \omega \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$, where the value of $x$ is _____.
question: A circular table is rotating with an angular velocity of $\omega \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$ about its axis (see figure). There is a smooth groove along a radial direction on the table. A steel ball is gently placed at a distance of $1 \mathrm{~m}$ on the groove. All the surfaces are smooth. If the radius of the table is $3 \mathrm{~m}$, the radial velocity of the ball w.r.t. the table at the time ball leaves the table is $x \sqrt{2} \omega \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$, where the value of $x$ is _____.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & a_c=\omega^2 x \\ & \frac{v d v}{d x}=\omega^2 x \\ & \int_0^v v d v=\int_1^3 \omega^2 x d x \\ & \frac{v^2}{2}=\omega^2\left[\frac{x^2}{2}\right] \\ & \frac{v^2}{2}=\frac{\omega^2}{2}\left[3^2-1^2\right] \\ & v=2 \sqrt{2} \omega \\ & x=2\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
A hollow spherical ball of uniform density rolls up a curved surface with an initial velocity 3 m s-1 (as shown in figure). Maximum height with respect to the initial position covered by it will be _____ cm
(take, g=10 m s-2)
question:
A hollow spherical ball of uniform density rolls up a curved surface with an initial velocity 3 m s-1 (as shown in figure). Maximum height with respect to the initial position covered by it will be _____ cm
(take, g=10 m s-2)
solution:
The total kinetic energy of the ball at the bottom of the curve can be calculated as follows:

K=12Mv2+12·23MR2ω2= 12Mv2+13MRω2= 12Mv2+13Mv2     ∵v=ωR= 56Mv2   ...1

The formula to calculate the potential energy acquired by the ball when it attains the maximum height is given by

U=Mgh   ...2

Equate equation (1) and (2) and simplify to obtain the required height.

Mgh=56Mv2⇒h=5v26g   ...3

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (3) to calculate the required height attained by the ball.
h=5×3 m s-126×10 m s-2= 0.75 m×100 cm1 m=75 cm

subject: Physics
q: A bullet of mass 200 g having initial kinetic energy 90 J is shot inside a long swimming pool as shown in the figure. If it's kinetic energy reduces to 40 J within 1 s, the minimum length of the pool, the bullet has to travel so that it completely comes to rest is
question: A bullet of mass 200 g having initial kinetic energy 90 J is shot inside a long swimming pool as shown in the figure. If it's kinetic energy reduces to 40 J within 1 s, the minimum length of the pool, the bullet has to travel so that it completely comes to rest is
solution:
Let us assume that the motion of the bullet is of constant retardation inside the water.

Initial kinetic energy 12mv02=90 J

⇒v0=30 m s-1

Let the retardation is a, then after 1 s

12m30-a×12=40 J

⇒30-a=20

⇒a=10 m s-2

Stopping distance for the constant retardation is given by,

l=u22a=30220=45 m.

Therefore, the minimum length of the pool required is 45 m.

subject: Physics
q: A block of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> slides over a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> on a horizontal surface. If the co-efficient of friction between the surfaces is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn></math> , then the work done against friction (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>) is:
question: A block of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> slides over a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> on a horizontal surface. If the co-efficient of friction between the surfaces is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn></math> , then the work done against friction (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>) is:
solution:
Given m=100 kg

s=10 m

μ=0.4

The frictional force is given by

 f=μmg=0.4×100×10=400 N

So, the work done by the force can be calculated as follows:

W=f×s=400×10=4000 J

subject: Physics
q: A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> is moving in a straight line with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi><msup><mi>x</mi><mfrac><mn>5</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></msup></math>. The work done by the net force during its displacement from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is  (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mfrac><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow><mn>2</mn></mfrac></msup><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>).
question: A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> is moving in a straight line with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi><msup><mi>x</mi><mfrac><mn>5</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></msup></math>. The work done by the net force during its displacement from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is  (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mfrac><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow><mn>2</mn></mfrac></msup><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>).
solution:
According to the work-energy theorem, work done by all forces will be is equal to the change in kinetic energy.

Wall=ΔK  

=12mb2x25-x15  =12×0.5×116×45=16 J

subject: Physics
q: A particle is acted upon by a force of constant magnitude which is always perpendicular to the velocity of the particle, the motion of the particle takes place in a plane. It follows that
question: A particle is acted upon by a force of constant magnitude which is always perpendicular to the velocity of the particle, the motion of the particle takes place in a plane. It follows that
options: it moves in a straight line.
solution: When a force of constant magnitude which is always perpendicular to the velocity of the particle acts on a particle, the work done and hence change in kinetic energy is zero.

question: A particle is released on a vertical smooth semicircular track from point X so that, OX makes angle θ  from the vertical (see figure). The normal reaction of the track on the particle vanishes at the point Y where OY makes an angle ϕ with the horizontal. Then
solution:
mgRcosθ-Rsinϕ=12mV2  .......(1)

On losing contact N=0⇒mgsinϕ=mV2R  ......(2)



⇒mgRcosθ-sinϕ=12mgRsinϕ

⇒2cosθ=3sinϕ

subject: Physics
q: The potential energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>U</mi></mfenced></math> of a diatomic molecule is a function dependent on <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> (interatomic distance) as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>U</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>α</mi><msup><mi>r</mi><mn>10</mn></msup></mfrac><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mi>β</mi><msup><mi>r</mi><mn>5</mn></msup></mfrac><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></math> where, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> are positive constants. The equilibrium distance between two atoms will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>α</mi></mrow><mi>β</mi></mfrac></mfenced><mfrac><mi>a</mi><mi>b</mi></mfrac></msup><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>=</mo></math>______ .
question: The potential energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>U</mi></mfenced></math> of a diatomic molecule is a function dependent on <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> (interatomic distance) as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>U</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>α</mi><msup><mi>r</mi><mn>10</mn></msup></mfrac><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mi>β</mi><msup><mi>r</mi><mn>5</mn></msup></mfrac><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></math> where, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> are positive constants. The equilibrium distance between two atoms will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>α</mi></mrow><mi>β</mi></mfrac></mfenced><mfrac><mi>a</mi><mi>b</mi></mfrac></msup><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>=</mo></math>______ .
solution:
For equilibrium

dUdr=0

-10αr11+5βr6=0

5βr6=10αr11

r5=2αβ

r5=2αβ

r=2αβ15a=1

subject: Physics
q: A small particle moves to position <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> from its initial position <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> under the action of force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>7</mn><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. The value of work done will be ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>.
question: A small particle moves to position <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> from its initial position <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> under the action of force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>7</mn><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. The value of work done will be ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>.
solution:
Work done by a constant force is given by W=ForceF→·Displacementr→, where, r→=r→2-r→1

Given here, r1→=5i^-2j^+k^ and r→2=2i^+3j^-4k^

So, W=F→·r→2-r→1

=(5i^+2j^+7k^)·(5i^-2j^+k^)-(2i^+3j^-4k^)

⇒W=40 J

subject: Physics
q: A person trying to lose weight by burning fat lifts a mass of 10 kg upto a height of 1m 1000 times. Assume that the potential energy lost each time he lowers the mass is dissipated. How much fat will he use up considering the work done only when the weight is lifted up? Fat supplies <math><mn>3.8</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>7</mn></mrow></msup></math> J of energy per kg which is converted to mechanical energy with a 20% efficiency rate. Take <math><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>9.8</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> :
question: A person trying to lose weight by burning fat lifts a mass of 10 kg upto a height of 1m 1000 times. Assume that the potential energy lost each time he lowers the mass is dissipated. How much fat will he use up considering the work done only when the weight is lifted up? Fat supplies <math><mn>3.8</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>7</mn></mrow></msup></math> J of energy per kg which is converted to mechanical energy with a 20% efficiency rate. Take <math><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>9.8</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> :
solution:
m=10kg, h=1m, 1000 times

Work done against gravity mgh

=10×1×9.8=98 J

In lifting 1000 times

=1000×98

=9.8×104 J

Fat burn with 20% efficiency =3.8×107 J×0.2

=7.6×106 J per kg

m=9.8×1047.6×106=1.289×10-2

=12.89×10-3 kg

subject: Physics
q: A force acts on a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi> </mi><mi>kg</mi></math> object so that its position is given as a function of time as <math><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><msup><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo></math> What is the work done by this force in first <math><mn>5</mn></math> seconds?
question: A force acts on a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi> </mi><mi>kg</mi></math> object so that its position is given as a function of time as <math><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><msup><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo></math> What is the work done by this force in first <math><mn>5</mn></math> seconds?
solution:
Here, the position of the object is x=3t2+5, therefore the velocity of the object will be,

v=dxdt=d3t2+5dt

v=6t+0

From the work-energy theorem, all the work done by the force acting on it will be equal to the change in its kinetic energy, i.e.,

W=KEt=5 s-KEt=0 s
W=12×2×6×52-12×2×6×02
W=900 J.

subject: Physics
q: A $1$ kg block subjected to two simultaneous forces $(2\hat{i} + 3\hat{j} + 4\hat{k})$ N and $(3\hat{i} - \hat{j} - 2\hat{k})$ N is moved a distance of $25$ m along $(3\hat{i} - 4\hat{j})$ direction. The work done in this process is _____ J.
question: A $1$ kg block subjected to two simultaneous forces $(2\hat{i} + 3\hat{j} + 4\hat{k})$ N and $(3\hat{i} - \hat{j} - 2\hat{k})$ N is moved a distance of $25$ m along $(3\hat{i} - 4\hat{j})$ direction. The work done in this process is _____ J.
solution:
The net force acting on the block is the vector sum of the two forces:

$\vec{F} = \vec{F}_1 + \vec{F}_2 = (2\hat{i} + 3\hat{j} + 4\hat{k}) + (3\hat{i} - \hat{j} - 2\hat{k}) = 5\hat{i} + 2\hat{j} + 2\hat{k}$ N

The block is moved a distance of $25$ m along the direction of the vector $3\hat{i} - 4\hat{j}$. The unit vector along this direction is:

$\hat{n} = \dfrac{3\hat{i} - 4\hat{j}}{\sqrt{3^2 + (-4)^2}} = \dfrac{3\hat{i} - 4\hat{j}}{5}$

The displacement vector $\vec{s}$ is the magnitude multiplied by the unit vector:

$\vec{s} = 25 \hat{n} = 25 \left( \dfrac{3\hat{i} - 4\hat{j}}{5} \right) = 15\hat{i} - 20\hat{j}$ m

The work done $W$ is the dot product of the net force and the displacement vector:

$W = \vec{F} \cdot \vec{s} = (5\hat{i} + 2\hat{j} + 2\hat{k}) \cdot (15\hat{i} - 20\hat{j})$

$W = (5 \times 15) + (2 \times -20) + (2 \times 0)$

$W = 75 - 40 = 35$ J

Answer: $35$

subject: Physics
q: A particle of mass $100 \mathrm{~g}$ is thrown vertically upwards with a speed of $5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. the work done by the force of gravity during the time the particle goes up is
question: A particle of mass $100 \mathrm{~g}$ is thrown vertically upwards with a speed of $5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. the work done by the force of gravity during the time the particle goes up is
solution: $-m g h=-m g\left(\frac{v^2}{2 g}\right)=-1.25 \mathrm{~J}$

subject: Physics
q: The potential energy function (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math> ) of a particle in a region of space is given as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>U</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>z</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>. Here <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>y</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi></math> are in meter. The magnitude of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> - component of force (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> ) acting on the particle at point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>3</mn><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is:
question: The potential energy function (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math> ) of a particle in a region of space is given as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>U</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>z</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>. Here <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>y</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi></math> are in meter. The magnitude of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> - component of force (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> ) acting on the particle at point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>3</mn><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is:
solution:
Given U=2x2+3y3+2z

The expression for the x-component of the force can be calculated as follows: 

Fx=-∂U∂x=-4x

Hence, at the point 1, 2, 3 m, the value of the force is given by

Fx=-4×1 N=4 N

subject: Physics
q: A particle of mass $m$ moves on a straight line with its velocity increasing with distance according to the equation $v=\alpha \sqrt{x}$, where $\alpha$ is a constant. The total work done by all the forces applied on the particle during its displacement from $x=0$ to $x=\mathrm{d}$, will be :
question: A particle of mass $m$ moves on a straight line with its velocity increasing with distance according to the equation $v=\alpha \sqrt{x}$, where $\alpha$ is a constant. The total work done by all the forces applied on the particle during its displacement from $x=0$ to $x=\mathrm{d}$, will be :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{v}=\alpha \sqrt{\mathrm{x}} \\ & \text { at } \mathrm{x}=0: \mathrm{v}=0 \\ & \& \text { at } \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{d} ; \mathrm{v}=\alpha \sqrt{\mathrm{d}} \\ & \text { W.D }=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}-\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{i}} \\ & \text { W.D }=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}(\alpha \sqrt{\mathrm{d}})^2-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}(0)^2 \\ & \Rightarrow \text { W.D }=\frac{\mathrm{m} \alpha^2 \mathrm{~d}}{2}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A particle moves in a straight line with retardation proportional to its displacement. Its loss of kinetic energy for any displacement $\mathrm{x}$ is proportional to
question: A particle moves in a straight line with retardation proportional to its displacement. Its loss of kinetic energy for any displacement $\mathrm{x}$ is proportional to
solution:
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}-\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{i}}=\frac{\mathrm{mk}}{2} \mathrm{x}^2$
$\mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{f}}-\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{i}} \propto \mathrm{x}^2$

subject: Physics
q: Two solids <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> respectively are moving with equal linear momentum. The ratio of their kinetic energies <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mfenced><mrow><mi>K</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>E</mi><mo>.</mo></mrow></mfenced><mi>A</mi></msub><mo>:</mo><msub><mfenced><mrow><mi>K</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>E</mi><mo>.</mo></mrow></mfenced><mi>B</mi></msub></math> will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>A</mi><mn>1</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo></math> so the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> will be _________.
question: Two solids <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> respectively are moving with equal linear momentum. The ratio of their kinetic energies <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mfenced><mrow><mi>K</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>E</mi><mo>.</mo></mrow></mfenced><mi>A</mi></msub><mo>:</mo><msub><mfenced><mrow><mi>K</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>E</mi><mo>.</mo></mrow></mfenced><mi>B</mi></msub></math> will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>A</mi><mn>1</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo></math> so the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> will be _________.
solution:
Kinetic energy K=P22m,PA=PB

K∝1 m

KAKB=mBmA

=21

subject: Physics
q: If momentum of a body is increased by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo>%</mo></math>, then its kinetic energy increases by :
question: If momentum of a body is increased by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo>%</mo></math>, then its kinetic energy increases by :
solution:
A body of mass m, moving with momentum p and kinetic energy K are related as follows,
K=p22m.
As the momentum of the body is increased by 20%, then its momentum becomes,
p+20100p=1.2 p
and the mass of the body remains unchanged. So, kinetic energy is directly related to the square of momentum. 

Hence, new kinetic energy K' will be,
K'=1.2 p22m

K'=1.44 K.

So, the percentage change in kinetic energy
=K'-KK×100 
=1.44K-KK×100
=1.44-1×100
=44%.

subject: Physics
q: A spherical body of mass 2 kg starting from rest acquires a kinetic energy of 10000 J at the end of 5th  second. The force acted on the body is _____ N.
question: A spherical body of mass 2 kg starting from rest acquires a kinetic energy of 10000 J at the end of 5th  second. The force acted on the body is _____ N.
solution:
Given kinetic energy, 12mv2=10000 J

⇒12×2×v2=10000

⇒  v2=10000

⇒  v=100 m s-1

Also,

⇒ v=at=a×5=100

⇒  a=20 m s-2

Therefore, force acting

F=ma=2×20=40 N

The energy released fission is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math>. Given that the Avogadro number, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>023</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>26</mn></msup></math> per kilo mole and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>eV</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>19</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>. The power output of the reactor is close to:
question: In a reactor, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">U</mi><none></none><mn>235</mn><mprescripts></mprescripts><mn>92</mn><none></none></mmultiscripts></math> fuel is fully used up in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn></math> days. The energy released fission is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math>. Given that the Avogadro number, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>023</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>26</mn></msup></math> per kilo mole and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>eV</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>19</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>. The power output of the reactor is close to:
solution:
P=Et

=2235×6.023×1026×200×1.6×10-1930×24×60×60=60 MW

subject: Physics
q: A force $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}=2 \hat{i}+\mathrm{b} \hat{j}+\hat{k}$ is applied on a particle and it undergoes a displacement $\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}$. What will be the value of $b$, if work done on the particle is zero.
question: A force $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}=2 \hat{i}+\mathrm{b} \hat{j}+\hat{k}$ is applied on a particle and it undergoes a displacement $\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}$. What will be the value of $b$, if work done on the particle is zero.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & w=0 \\ & \therefore \quad \vec{F} \cdot S=0 \\ & (2 \hat{i}+b \hat{j}+\hat{k}) \cdot(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-\hat{k})=0 \\ & 2-2 b-1=0 \\ & b=\frac{1}{2}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> starts moving from rest along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo></math>axis so that its velocity varies as <math><mi>v</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi><msqrt><mi>s</mi></msqrt></math> where <math><mi>a</mi></math> is a constant and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>s</mi></math> is the distance covered by the body. The total work done by all the forces acting on the body in the first <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> second after the start of the motion is
question: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> starts moving from rest along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo></math>axis so that its velocity varies as <math><mi>v</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi><msqrt><mi>s</mi></msqrt></math> where <math><mi>a</mi></math> is a constant and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>s</mi></math> is the distance covered by the body. The total work done by all the forces acting on the body in the first <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> second after the start of the motion is
solution:
v=as=dsdt

⇒dss=a.dt

⇒2s=at

⇒s=a2t24, v=dsdt=a2t2

⇒Wall=∆K=Kf-Ki

⇒Wall=12mv22-v12

⇒v1=0, v2=a2t2

⇒Wall=12ma2t22-0

⇒Wall=18ma4t2

⇒Wall=∆K

subject: Physics
q: A spring of force constant $800 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$ has an extension of $5 \mathrm{~cm}$. The work done is extending it from $5 \mathrm{~cm}$ to $15 \mathrm{~cm}$ is
question: A spring of force constant $800 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$ has an extension of $5 \mathrm{~cm}$. The work done is extending it from $5 \mathrm{~cm}$ to $15 \mathrm{~cm}$ is
solution: $\mathrm{W}=\int_{x_1}^{x_2} F d x=\int_{x_1}^{x_2} K x d x=K\left[\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_{x_1}^{x_2}=\frac{K}{2}\left[x_2^2-x_1^2\right]=\frac{800}{2}\left[(0.15)^2-(0.05)^2\right]=8 \mathrm{~J}$
